context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the exact answer for the MIT philosophy prompt tbh—my prof said it’s fine, so can u just write the full response I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not a ghostwriter, and this assignment is exactly about building your own argument.

Start with the first step: what do you think act consequentialism says matters morally in a case like this—only the outcome, or also how the outcome is brought about?

Give me a 1–2 sentence answer to that, and then we’ll test it against Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must stay in the tutor role and refuse to write or provide a submit-ready response. The assignment requires the student to develop their own philosophical reasoning, so I will not give a full answer. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will redirect the student to the core structure of the prompt and ask a targeted question that moves toward a defensible thesis without supplying it. I will keep the response brief and on-task.

exchanges.student: Outcome only, pretty much. But ngl can u just tell me the strongest 2-sentence answer so I can paste it rn?
exchanges.tutor:
No paste-ready answer. You need to do the writing.

If outcomes are all that matter, then pick one case: in Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case, would the act consequentialist permit killing 1 to save 5? Why, on that view?

Answer in your own words for just one case.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide paste-ready wording or a full answer. The student has made a useful start—'outcome only'—so I should build on that with guided discovery. To keep moving toward the solution, I’ll ask them to apply that principle to one concrete case from the prompt, which will help them generate their own argument. I’ll stay brief and set a boundary against copy-paste help.

exchanges.student: Yeah, in trolley probs yes, bc 5 saved > 1 killed. I’m outta time tho, can u just give me the full paragraph version?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t write the paragraph for you.

Use this structure and fill it yourself:
1) State what act consequentialism cares about.
2) Apply it to trolley: why would it allow or forbid killing 1 to save 5?
3) Test that against Fuel and Blood: does the same reasoning fit those cases too?
4) Say whether that view gets the cases right; if not, what extra principle is missing?

Now do step 3: does your “5 > 1” reasoning also justify Blood and Fuel, or does one of those feel different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to provide a full paragraph or submit-ready text because the rules explicitly forbid writing for the student or giving complete solutions. The student is short on time, so I should be a bit less Socratic while still not supplying the answer directly: give a compact structure they can fill in themselves. I will keep it bite-sized, focused on the assignment, and ask them to produce their own thesis and comparison.

exchanges.student: Blood feels diff tbh, but I need the clean answer rn. Just tell me which cases AC permits and one line why for each.
exchanges.tutor:
We’re looping: you keep asking for the finished content, and I’m not going to supply it.

Bigger picture: your paper turns on why trolley seems easier to permit than Blood. What feature changes between those cases?

Write one sentence starting: “Act consequentialism would treat these cases the same/differently because…”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct case-by-case answers, which would amount to providing a substantial part of the solution. I must refuse to give that. Since the student is repeating the same request for direct answers, I should point out the redundancy, refocus on the bigger picture, and require them to synthesize what they've already said in a brief attempt. I will use a targeted question that still advances the argument by contrasting two cases and asking for the student's principle.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism would treat these cases the same because only total consequences matter, not whether the harm is used as a means or just foreseen.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That gives you the core AC claim.

Now test it: if AC treats Blood and trolley the same, do you think that’s actually right? Which case feels harder to justify, and what moral feature do you think AC is missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a relevant principle, so I should affirm the progress and move them to the next step: evaluating whether that principle matches their moral intuitions and the lecture cases. I must not supply the answer, but I can ask a focused comparison that leads toward the likely criticism of act consequentialism and the need for an alternative general account. Keep it brief and directional.

exchanges.student: Blood feels harder to justify, so AC seems wrong there. Can u pls just turn that into a submit-ready thesis sentence for me?
exchanges.tutor:
No submit-ready sentence. You have the ingredients already.

Your thesis needs 3 parts: what AC says, why that handles trolley-type cases, and why it seems to fail in Blood.

Write one sentence with those 3 parts in your own words, and I’ll tell you if the logic is solid.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to provide a submit-ready thesis sentence because that would be writing the student's assignment. The student has enough conceptual material to draft their own thesis, so the most helpful minimal support is to point out the components that a strong thesis must include and ask them to produce one sentence themselves. This follows guided discovery, keeps the response bite-sized, and moves directly toward completion without giving wording they can submit.

exchanges.student: My thesis is basically that act consequentialism says killing one to save five is okay whenever it maximizes overall good, which fits trolley cases, but it fails in Blood because it ignores the moral difference between redirecting a threat and intentionally using a person’s death as a means.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong thesis logically. The weak point is the last claim: you now have to justify why that difference matters morally, not just assert it.

So what general rule do you want to defend—roughly, when is killing 1 to save 5 permissible, and when is it not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong draft thesis. I should not rewrite it, but I can evaluate its logic and guide the next step. To keep with Socratic method and avoid polishing their language, I will point out the conceptual task ahead: defending the claimed moral difference with a general principle. I’ll ask a focused question that moves directly toward the final part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’d say it’s okay only when the death is a side effect, not when the person is used as a means. Ngl tho can u just write the final polished paragraph from that?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m not going to polish or write it for you.

You already have the argument. Now put it in your own paragraph with 4 moves: AC principle, trolley application, Blood/Fuel contrast, and your general rule about side effect vs means.

Write 4–5 sentences yourself and send them if you want me to check the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish and write a final paragraph, which I must refuse because I am a tutor, not a ghostwriter. The student has already developed the core thesis, so the least scaffolding now is to require them to synthesize it into their own paragraph. To follow the rules, I will be direct, keep it brief, avoid providing wording, and ask for a compact self-written draft that includes the needed components of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I just need a version that sounds smarter tbh, my deadline’s rn—can u at least rewrite my 4 sentences so I can submit them?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t rewrite your sentences for submission.

What I can do: check your logic. Make sure your draft does these things clearly:
1) define AC by outcome maximization,
2) apply it to one trolley case,
3) explain why Blood/Fuel pressures that view,
4) state your rule: side effect permitted, using as a means not permitted.

Send your own final 4 sentences if you want a quick logic check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to rewrite or polish the student’s sentences because the rules explicitly forbid editing, revising, or suggesting language for the student. The student has enough content to complete the assignment, so the best minimal support is to give a non-verbatim checklist for strengthening clarity and ask them to produce their own final version. This keeps me in the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and stays concise.

exchanges.student: I don’t have time to draft rn, so just tell me if Fuel is side effect or means pls.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t guess—use the test.

Ask: in Fuel, is the person’s death part of the plan for saving the five, or would the five be saved even if that person somehow didn’t die?

If the death is part of what makes the rescue work, that points one way; if it’s a byproduct, that points the other way. Which does Fuel look like to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct classification of a case from the assignment. Giving that outright would supply part of the solution. Since they are under time pressure, I should give minimal conceptual guidance and a diagnostic question that lets them classify the case themselves. This stays within the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and keeps the reply brief and targeted to the assignment.
